content.text: A webhook trigger fires when an external service pushes an HTTP POST to your endpoint on a specific event — a GitHub pull request opened, a Stripe payment failed, a PagerDuty alert created. The endpoint must respond within the provider's timeout (usually 3–10 seconds), so the pattern is always: verify the signature, enqueue the payload, return 200. An async worker pulls from the queue and runs the agent at its own pace.

attrs.alt: Webhook-triggered agent lifecycle: source → receiver (verify, 200, enqueue) → queue → agent worker → outputs

content.text: Every serious webhook provider signs its requests with an HMAC-SHA256 or similar signature in a header. Verification protects you from forged requests — anyone who knows your endpoint URL can POST to it without signing. Always verify before processing.

The webhook endpoint does the minimum work required to acknowledge the request: verify the signature, deserialise the payload, push it to a queue, and return 200. No LLM calls, no database queries, no synchronous network calls.

import boto3, json, os
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, BackgroundTasks, HTTPException

app = FastAPI()
sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
QUEUE_URL = os.environ["WEBHOOK_QUEUE_URL"]

async def enqueue(payload: dict, event_type: str, source: str) -> None:
    sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MessageBody=json.dumps({
            "source": source,
            "event_type": event_type,
            "payload": payload,
        }),
        MessageAttributes={
            "source": {"DataType": "String", "StringValue": source},
            "event_type": {"DataType": "String", "StringValue": event_type},
        },
    )

@app.post("/webhooks/github")
async def github_handler(request: Request, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks) -> dict:
    body = await request.body()
    # verify_github(body, request.headers.get("X-Hub-Signature-256", ""))  # shown above
    event = request.headers.get("X-GitHub-Event", "unknown")
    payload = json.loads(body)
    background_tasks.add_task(enqueue, payload, event, "github")
    return {"ok": True}

@app.post("/webhooks/stripe")
async def stripe_handler(request: Request, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks) -> dict:
    body = await request.body()
    payload = json.loads(body)
    background_tasks.add_task(enqueue, payload, payload.get("type", "unknown"), "stripe")
    return {"ok": True}

The worker pulls events from the queue and dispatches them to event-specific agent functions. Each function receives the raw payload and decides what the agent should do — analyse, summarise, comment, alert, or update a record.

import boto3, json, time, logging
from anthropic import Anthropic

sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
client = Anthropic()
QUEUE_URL = "..."
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

def handle_github_pr_opened(payload: dict) -> None:
    pr = payload.get("pull_request", {})
    title = pr.get("title", "")
    body_text = pr.get("body", "") or ""
    author = pr.get("user", {}).get("login", "unknown")

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"PR opened by {author}:\nTitle: {title}\nDescription: {body_text[:800]}\n\n"
                "Is this PR description clear and complete? Flag any missing sections in 2 sentences."
            )
        }]
    )
    feedback = resp.content[0].text
    logging.info(f"PR #{pr.get('number')} feedback: {feedback}")
    # In production: post as a GitHub PR comment via the GitHub REST API

def handle_stripe_payment_failed(payload: dict) -> None:
    data = payload.get("data", {}).get("object", {})
    customer_id = data.get("customer", "unknown")
    amount = data.get("amount", 0) / 100
    logging.warning(f"Payment failed: customer={customer_id}, amount=${amount:.2f}")
    # In production: look up customer in CRM, draft a recovery email

HANDLERS = {
    ("github", "pull_request:opened"): handle_github_pr_opened,
    ("stripe", "payment_intent.payment_failed"): handle_stripe_payment_failed,
}

def worker_loop():
    while True:
        resp = sqs.receive_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, MaxNumberOfMessages=10, WaitTimeSeconds=20)
        for msg in resp.get("Messages", []):
            try:
                body = json.loads(msg["Body"])
                source = body["source"]
                event_type = body["event_type"]
                payload = body["payload"]
                key = (source, event_type)
                handler = HANDLERS.get(key)
                if handler:
                    handler(payload)
                else:
                    logging.debug(f"No handler for {key}")
                sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, ReceiptHandle=msg["ReceiptHandle"])
            except Exception as e:
                logging.error(f"Worker error: {e}")
                # Do not delete — SQS will redeliver after visibility timeout

if __name__ == "__main__":
    worker_loop()
Scale workers horizontally — Lambda or ECS
Webhook providers need a public URL to deliver events. During local development, ngrok creates a secure tunnel from a public URL to your local machine, so you can test webhooks without deploying.

# Install ngrok: https://ngrok.com/download
# Start your local FastAPI server
uvicorn main:app --port 8000

# In a second terminal, create a tunnel
ngrok http 8000

# ngrok outputs a public URL like: https://abc123.ngrok.io
# Register this URL in the webhook provider's dashboard:
# GitHub: Settings → Webhooks → Add webhook
# Stripe: Dashboard → Developers → Webhooks → Add endpoint
# Payload URL: https://abc123.ngrok.io/webhooks/github
Unit tests should replay saved webhook payloads against the handler without going through an actual HTTP request. Keep a
directory of real webhook JSON samples captured during development.

# tests/test_github_webhook.py
import json
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from main import app  # your FastAPI app

client = TestClient(app)

def load_fixture(name: str) -> bytes:
    with open(f"tests/fixtures/{name}.json", "rb") as f:
        return f.read()

def make_github_sig(body: bytes, secret: str) -> str:
    import hashlib, hmac
    return "sha256=" + hmac.new(secret.encode(), body, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()

def test_github_pr_opened():
    body = load_fixture("github_pr_opened")
    sig = make_github_sig(body, "test-secret")

    with patch.dict("os.environ", {"GITHUB_WEBHOOK_SECRET": "test-secret"}):
        with patch("main.sqs") as mock_sqs:
            resp = client.post(
                "/webhooks/github",
                content=body,
                headers={"X-Hub-Signature-256": sig, "X-GitHub-Event": "pull_request"},
            )
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    assert mock_sqs.send_message.called

def test_invalid_signature_rejected():
    body = load_fixture("github_pr_opened")
    with patch.dict("os.environ", {"GITHUB_WEBHOOK_SECRET": "test-secret"}):
        resp = client.post(
            "/webhooks/github",
            content=body,
            headers={"X-Hub-Signature-256": "sha256=invalid", "X-GitHub-Event": "pull_request"},
        )
    assert resp.status_code == 401

Webhook providers guarantee at-least-once delivery — the same event may arrive twice (network retry, provider-side bug). Agent side effects (tickets created, emails sent, records updated) must be idempotent. The standard fix is to track the provider's event ID and skip re-processing.

import redis, json

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)
PROCESSED_TTL = 86400 * 3  # 3 days

def is_processed(event_id: str) -> bool:
    return r.exists(f"webhook_event:{event_id}") == 1

def mark_processed(event_id: str) -> None:
    r.set(f"webhook_event:{event_id}", "1", ex=PROCESSED_TTL)

def idempotent_handler(event: dict) -> str | None:
    event_id = (
        event.get("id")                   # GitHub, Stripe include an 'id' field
        or event.get("guid")
        or event.get("delivery_id")
    )
    if not event_id:
        import hashlib, json as _json
        event_id = hashlib.sha256(_json.dumps(event, sort_keys=True).encode()).hexdigest()

    if is_processed(event_id):
        return None  # Skip — already handled

    # Run the agent
    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarise event: {json.dumps(event)[:500]}"}]
    )
    result = resp.content[0].text

    # Mark only after success — if we crash here the event is re-delivered and re-processed,
    # which is safer than losing it silently.
    mark_processed(event_id)
    return result
A single webhook event often needs multiple independent responses — a
event should trigger a code review agent, a size-check agent, and a label-assignment agent simultaneously. Use SNS fan-out or a topic-per-purpose pattern to run them in parallel.

import boto3, json

sns = boto3.client("sns", region_name="us-east-1")
sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")

# SNS fan-out: one SNS topic delivers to multiple SQS queues
# Each queue has its own consumer (agent worker) that handles independently.
# Set up in CDK/Terraform:
# SNS topic "github-pr-events"
#   → SQS "pr-code-review-queue"   (consumed by code-review agent)
#   → SQS "pr-size-check-queue"    (consumed by size-check agent)
#   → SQS "pr-label-queue"         (consumed by label-assignment agent)

GITHUB_PR_TOPIC = "arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789:github-pr-events"

def publish_pr_event(payload: dict) -> str:
    resp = sns.publish(
        TopicArn=GITHUB_PR_TOPIC,
        Message=json.dumps(payload),
        MessageAttributes={
            "action": {"DataType": "String", "StringValue": payload.get("action", "unknown")}
        },
    )
    return resp["MessageId"]

# Each queue's consumer receives the same event independently.
# SNS delivers a copy to each subscribed SQS queue simultaneously.
# If the code-review agent fails, the size-check agent is unaffected.
Maintain a registry of handled event types so it's easy to audit which events your system responds to and add new handlers without modifying the dispatch logic.
— running the agent inside the webhook handler causes 502 timeouts when the provider stops waiting. Always return 200 first and hand off to a queue.
— unverified endpoints accept forged payloads from anyone. One line of HMAC verification prevents this entirely.
— providers retry on network errors. Without deduplication on the event ID, a transient failure doubles your agent's side effects.
— a dispatch table of if/elif strings breaks as the catalog grows. Use the decorator-based registry pattern to keep handlers co-located with their event types.
— events piling up in the DLQ are invisible until someone notices a gap in processing. Set a CloudWatch alarm on DLQ depth > 0.

Webhook triggers activate an agent when an external service posts an event. The receiver verifies the HMAC signature, enqueues the raw payload, and returns 200 immediately — it never runs the agent synchronously. A separate worker pulls events from the queue, dispatches to a per-event-type handler, and either deletes the message on success or leaves it for retry. Use SQS with
and a DLQ to handle permanent failures. Test handlers with saved fixture payloads; use ngrok for local development.
